coredata.dc:title: Experimental and numerical study of internal pressure of lithium-ion batteries under overheating
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Haodong
Gulsoy, Begum
Barai, Anup
Nakhanivej, Puritut
Loveridge, Melanie J.
Marco, James
coredata.dc:description: The safety of lithium-ion batteries remains one of the major public concerns as electric vehicle fires are frequently reported. Currently, understanding the thermal runaway characteristics and mechanisms via the combination of thermal runaway modelling and tests using in-situ internal pressure measurements in 21,700-format lithium-ion cells has rarely been reported. This work presents a lumped thermal runaway model and thermal runaway experimental results with instrumented 21,700-format cells (equipped with pressure/temperature sensors) to understand thermal runaway characteristics and mechanisms based on the evolution of the internal pressure. The internal temperature recorded by an embedded thermocouple reached up to 1008.9 °C. The average internal pressures for the first peak (vent disc pressure) and the second peak (during thermal runaway) were 20.43 bar and 18.27 bar, respectively. The thermal runaway mechanisms were further explained based on experimental results and the developed model. Finally, the influence of both vent area and gas generation on the evolution of internal pressure was investigated based on the developed model. A larger opening in the bottom vent would decrease the internal pressure, which would reduce the risk of sidewall rupture. The study addressed the key role of bottom vent played during thermal runaway especially the evolution of internal pressure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery safety
Thermal runaway modelling
Internal pressure
Internal temperature
Thermal abuse

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Innovate UK, the Aerospace Technology Institute (Grant Number: TS/V028049/1). We would like also to acknowledge the Faraday Institution's SafeBatt Project (FIRG061) for partial funding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cell autopsy. (a) a whole cell without a plastic wrapper, (b) cell cap before removing from the positive tabs, (c) jellyroll, (d) top view of jellyroll, (e) bottom view of jellyroll, (f) positive terminal, (g) cell cap disassembly, from left to right, they are vent disc (top disc), plastic insert between top disc and bottom disc, bottom disc, plastic insert between cell housing and discs, and upper outer jacket (upper part of cell housing) (h) cell cap, (i) cell base (inside view), there are two disconnected semicircular scorings at the base, and (j) cell housing.
Diagrams of the experimental steps: (a) cell autopsy, (b) ARC test, (c) external heating test, and test setup of (d) ARC test, and (e) external heating test.
The flow chart of the model.
Surface temperature and temperature rise rate of the cells. (a) and (d) for Test 1, (b) and (e) for Test 2, and (c) and (f) for Test 3.
Comparison between surface temperature and internal temperature and their temperature rise rate in Test 3. (a) and (b) temperature, (c) and (d) temperature rise rate.
Surface temperature, cell voltage, and internal pressure of external heating tests. (a) and (d) for Test 4, (b) and (e) for Test 5, and (c) and (f) for Test 6.
Characteristics of gases, sparks, and flame during thermal runaway (Test 5).
(a) Comparison of middle surface temperature and internal pressure between test and model (Test 6), (b) effect of the vent area and (c) effect of gas generation.

Battery morphologies after thermal runaway. (a) Test 4, whole cell, top cover, bottom (middle hole for connecting the pressure sensor), bottom from the side view (from left to right), (b) Test 5, and (c) Test (6).
Influence of the valve pressure on the internal pressure.
Summary of pertinent literature within the domain.

No, temperature results from reference [15], internal pressure results before venting from reference, but cannot access
No, temperature results from reference [15] and others

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of external heating test results.
Summary of vent area for each test after thermal runaway.

head.ce:title: Experimental and numerical study of internal pressure of lithium-ion batteries under overheating

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Warwick Manufacturing Group, University of Warwick, Coventry CV4 7AL, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Warwick Manufacturing Group, University of Warwick, Coventry, CV4 7AL, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The safety of lithium-ion batteries remains one of the major public concerns as electric vehicle fires are frequently reported. Currently, understanding the thermal runaway characteristics and mechanisms via the combination of thermal runaway modelling and tests using in-situ internal pressure measurements in 21,700-format lithium-ion cells has rarely been reported. This work presents a lumped thermal runaway model and thermal runaway experimental results with instrumented 21,700-format cells (equipped with pressure/temperature sensors) to understand thermal runaway characteristics and mechanisms based on the evolution of the internal pressure. The internal temperature recorded by an embedded thermocouple reached up to 1008.9 °C. The average internal pressures for the first peak (vent disc pressure) and the second peak (during thermal runaway) were 20.43 bar and 18.27 bar, respectively. The thermal runaway mechanisms were further explained based on experimental results and the developed model. Finally, the influence of both vent area and gas generation on the evolution of internal pressure was investigated based on the developed model. A larger opening in the bottom vent would decrease the internal pressure, which would reduce the risk of sidewall rupture. The study addressed the key role of bottom vent played during thermal runaway especially the evolution of internal pressure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: reaction heat per unit mass (J/kg) or height (m)

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the physical size and energy density of commercial lithium-ion cells have continued to increase. Examples include the 21,700-format (diameter: 21 mm and height: 70 mm) and 4680-format (diameter: 46 mm and height: 80 mm) cylindrical cells commercially used in electric vehicles (EVs), replacing the common 18,650-format (diameter: 18 mm and height: 65 mm) variant. There is more volumetric energy stored in a larger format cell for the same chemistry. For example, the energy stored in a typical 21,700-format cell is higher by around 50 % compared to 18,650-format cells [1]. While both cell formats adopt the same housing materials and wall thicknesses, the higher specific energy in 21,700-format cells may lead to greater internal pressure and faster temperature rise during thermal runaway. The heightened internal pressure and rapid heat increase can result in more intense thermal runaway behaviour, such as violent jet fires and sidewall rupture [2]. This behaviour is less common in lower-energy-density 18,650-format cells. Since the strength of the casing material significantly degrades at high temperatures [3], the higher temperatures expected in 21,700-format cells compared to 18,650-format cells increase the likelihood of sidewall rupture in 21,700-format cells. Previous research has shown that sidewall rupture occurs more frequently in 21,700-format cells [4] than in 18,650-format cells [5]. Following this trend, the potential impact of thermal runaway is potentially larger for 4680 and other large-format prismatic cells. Although battery technology is rapidly evolving, the risk of thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries will not be fully eliminated, but there remains the need to understand the thermal runaway mechanisms and behaviours in larger format cells.

Thermal runaway can be explained by considering a series of chain reactions [6] and the balance between heat generation and heat dissipation [7]. To characterise the thermal runaway behaviours including temperature, pressure, voltage, mass loss, gases, flame, and internal structure in a cell, advanced equipment or instrumented cells or both were adopted. For example, Finegan et al. [8] adopted a high-speed synchrotron X-ray computed tomography and radiography to track the evolution of internal structural damage within 18,650-format cells. Chen et al. [9] used a high-speed camera to monitor the evolution of fire in 21,700-format cells. Mei et al. [10] adopted an optical fibre sensor to monitor the evolution of internal temperature and pressure in 18,650-format cells. Based on hundreds of accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC) tests on lithium-ion batteries of different formats and chemistries, Feng et al. [11] summarised the three characteristic temperatures including the onset temperature, trigger temperature and maximum temperature to understand the temperature features during the thermal runaway event. However, there is a very limited experimental study in 21,700-format cells to report the internal pressure features during thermal runaway. This is because it is difficult to make this measurement in a cell due to a very narrow internal space and the high temperatures (sometimes in excess of 1000 °C), violent combustion, and complex turbulence associated with thermal runaway.

Numerical studies have been conducted by researchers to investigate the evolution of internal pressure in 18,650-format cells. For example, Coman et al. [12,13] developed a mathematical model to predict the internal pressure before venting and the predicted internal pressure was experimentally validated within a 18,650-format lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) /graphite cells with a dimethyl carbonate (DMC) electrolyte. The work provides a framework for the development of a thermal runaway model with a venting process. Following this, Ostanek et al. [14] developed a detailed lumped thermal runaway model by considering the vapourization of electrolytes after venting, which is important to understand the heat loss taken away by the electrolyte vapour prior to thermal runaway occurring. Their work was built on the tests of 18,650-format lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxides (NMC)/graphite cells conducted by Golubkov et al. [15]. Note that the gas released from experiments was collected and the chamber pressure rather than the internal pressure of the cell was recorded in their study [15]. Mao et al. [16] developed a lumped model to predict the gas jet flow and fire dynamics of 18,650-format lithium-ion cells. Kong et al. [17] and He et al. [18] also developed a numerical model to calculate the temperature and internal pressure evolution of 18,650-format lithium-ion cells. The venting and subsequent combustion were simulated by coupling conjugate heat transfer with computational fluid dynamics (CFD) [17]. Huang et al. [19] developed a three-dimensional single-cell thermal runaway and gas diffusion model to study the first venting process inside a closed box based on 21,700-format cells equipped with a pressure sensor under external heating conditions. However, their model is limited and cannot account for changes in internal pressure after the first venting. In addition, other CFD studies on thermal runaway modelling can be referenced in the literature [20–24]. As can be seen, there is very limited work on the thermal runaway modelling with a venting process in 21,700-format cells. Table 1 summarises the pertinent literature within the domain and highlights those facets that are complementary to this study. In addition, few studies have considered the effect of vent area on the thermal runaway model. The vent plays a key role in relieving the internal pressure and preventing the sidewall rupture of a cell [4,25].

Similar limitations apply to in-situ internal pressure measurements inside 21,700, 18,650 and 4680-format lithium-ion cells, all are rarely reported. This work aims to fill the gap in thermal runaway modelling and internal pressure measurements of 21,700-format cells and deepen our understanding of battery thermal runaway mechanisms and internal pressure evolution during cell failure.

In this work, 21,700-format cells were modified using previously reported cell instrumentation methodology [27,28], and equipped with pressure sensors coupled at the cell bottom or negative end. These were used to track the evolution of the internal pressure during thermal runaway. The data was used to develop and parameterise a lumped thermal runaway model to better understand the evolution of internal pressure during cell failure. Cell disassembly to obtain the mass of cell components was performed to parameterise the initial model. The ARC test is used to extract reaction kinetic parameters for the model. The details of the external heating tests used for internal pressure characterisation and model validation are outlined in Section 2. Section 3 briefly presents the governing equations, initial conditions, and model implementation strategy. Section 4 shows experimental results and further validation and discusses the influences of the vent area and gas generation on internal cell pressure. Conclusions and further work are presented in Section 5.

Commercial cylindrical 21,700-format cells with LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA) cathode and graphite and SiO x anode were used in this work. The rated capacity of the cell is 4.0 Ah and the nominal voltage of the cell is 3.60 V. A total of eight cells were used in this research, three cells for the ARC test, three cells for the external heating test and two cells were disassembled to obtain cell component parameters to support model development. Except for the one disassembled cell, all the cells were fully charged to 4.2 V with a constant charging current (1C) and constant voltage procedure (4.2 V, cutoff current 200 mA) before testing commenced.

One fully discharged cell was disassembled within an argon-filled glove box (Mbraun – O2 and H2O < 0.1 ppm), as shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a shows the whole cell without a plastic wrapper and Fig. 1b shows the cell cap before removing from the positive tabs. Fig. 1c-e shows the jellyroll, the top view of the jellyroll, and the bottom view of the jellyroll, respectively. Fig. 1f shows the positive terminal. Fig. 1g and h shows components of the cell cap and itself, respectively. The positive terminal is directly connected to a vent disc, without a positive temperature coefficient (PTC) disc positioned beneath it. There are two safety vents which are in the cell cap and base. As shown in Fig. 1i, two disconnected semicircular scorings are observed at the base, which is used to generate a bottom vent and release the internal pressure when the opening of the vent disc is not enough to release the internal pressure. However, it is noteworthy that the exact operation is not documented with the cell's manufacturer data sheet. In addition, there is no mandrel in this cell.

To measure the mass of the electrolyte in the cell, another fully discharged cell was transferred to the glove box for disassembly. The cap was first removed, and the cap and the rest of the cell were placed in a glove box for one month to allow the electrolyte to evaporate fully. Following this, the jellyroll was disassembled and separators, cathode with current collector, and anode with current collector were placed in a vacuum oven (Binder – VD 53 vacuum drying oven) at 60 °C for 5 days to dry the remaining electrolyte. The mass of battery components was measured before and after drying using an analytical balance (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland) with an accuracy of ±0.1 mg. The diagram of step-by-step experimental procedures is shown in Fig. 2a. The measured parameters obtained from this process are listed in Table 2 .

An extended volume accelerating rate calorimeter (EV-ARC) (THT, UK) was used to investigate the surface and internal temperature of the cell. Three tests were conducted, each with different sensor configurations. The cell had no internal sensor in the first test while the cell was equipped with an internal pressure sensor and an internal temperature sensor in the second test and the third test, respectively. The pressure sensor (EPT3100-A-10000-B-1-6V-A, Variohm) has an accuracy of ±0.5 % of full scale across its 100-bar measurement range. The embedded R-type thermocouple operates up to 1750 °C, with a measurement error of ±5 °C at 1000 °C. The influence of embedded sensors on cell performance is negligible [27,28]. The diagram of experimental procedures is shown in Fig. 2b. The standard heat-wait-seek approach was used to heat a cell to thermal runaway. The key testing parameters for the ARC setting were as follows. The end temperature was 305 °C, the step rate of temperature increase was 5 °C and the sensitivity of temperature rise rate was 0.02 °C/min. The starting temperature was set as 50 °C and the wait time was 40 min for Tests 1 and 2, and 60 °C and 30 min for Test 3, respectively. The higher starting temperature and shorter waiting time in Test 3 were adopted to save testing time without influencing the results. The sampling rate of ARC changes automatically when the temperature of the tracking thermocouple changes quickly. As shown in Fig. 2d, an instrumented cell, equipped with a temperature sensor at the base of the cell was utilised. The tip of the sensor was positioned centrally within the cell. The cell is mounted on a steel frame with two chrome-plated pipe stand-off brackets. A tracking thermocouple was placed at the centre of the cell surface and wrapped in high-temperature-resistant glass cloth tape to ensure it remained securely in place throughout the test.

Three external heating tests were conducted to investigate the evolution of internal pressure. The diagram of experimental procedures is shown in Fig. 2c. The instrumented cells equipped with a pressure sensor at the base of the cell were prepared and the detailed process of making the instrumented cells can refer to our previous study [27]. A metal coil made of nickel chrome alloy with a thickness of 0.35 mm was used to heat the cell in this work. The metal coil was first firmly attached to the lower part of the cell, positioned 5 mm above the base, with a 2.0 cm wide high-temperature-resistant double-sided adhesive tape, wound around the cell for 6 turns, with a spacing of 2.5 mm between each turn. Next, the metal coil was wrapped with a 2.0 cm wide glass cloth tape with a silicone adhesive. Three K-type thermocouples with a measuring accuracy of ±1.5 °C were placed on the cell. Two of them were located at the cell surface, one was in the middle of the cell and the other was 10 mm from the top of the cell. The last thermocouple was attached to the glass cloth tape. After placing thermocouples, an insulation sheet was used to wrap the cell to reduce the heat transfer between the cell and the air. A voltage sensor was used to record the cell voltage during tests by connecting the positive and negative terminals. Finally, the cell with the pressure sensor, as shown in Fig. 2e, was mounted on the desk with a G-shape clamp and black nylon cable ties. All thermocouples and the voltage sensor were connected to a USB data logger (TC-08, Pico Technology) and controlled via PicoLog software (PicoLog 6.2.11, Pico Technology) on a laptop, recording data at a sampling rate of 10 Hz. The pressure sensor was connected to a separate data logger (NI 9220, cDAQ-9178, National Instruments), logging at 10 kHz to capture rapid internal pressure changes. A high-definition (HD) camera (Logitech) recording at 30 fps was positioned 1.0 m from the cell to capture thermal runaway behaviour. Power to the metal coil was supplied by a power source (HMP4040, Rohde & Schwarz) set to 10 V and connected to the coil with two cables.

In this work, a lumped model to simulate thermal runaway with a venting process was developed and validated by an external heating test. Fig. 3 shows a computational flow chart of the model. As shown in Fig. 3, the liquid electrolyte and its vapour are in a state of vapour-liquid equilibrium before the internal pressure of the cell is less than the threshold of vent pressure. The model firstly calculates the cell temperature and then the partial pressure of electrolyte vapour, followed by the calculation of the mass of vapour generation and gas mixture generation and their volume fraction. The partial pressure generated by the gas mixture is then calculated, before finally calculating the total pressure of the electrolyte vapour and gas mixture, until the internal pressure exceeds the threshold value. This is followed by determining whether the flow is subsonic or choked, according to the relationship between the internal pressure and ambient pressure. The dynamic parameters of gases flowing through the vent valve are calculated. The last step is to calculate the heat generation, cell temperature, and partial pressure of electrolyte vapour and gas mixture - the detailed formulas can be seen in Section 3.1.3.

Additional assumptions, such as the constant heat capacity of the cell for this lumped model are made to simplify the calculation [12–14,18]. These include:
1)
The electrolyte vapour and the gas mixture are thoroughly mixed before being released.
2)
The internal pressure within the cell is evenly distributed, and the gas temperature is approximately equal to the volume-averaged temperature of the cell components.
3)
The venting process is considered as an isentropic nozzle flow process and the changes in kinetic and potential energy are neglected.
4)
The heat capacity of cell components remains unchanged during thermal runaway.
5)
The cell temperature is not affected by heat from flame and smoke.

Compared to the previous model, our model offers two key advantages: (1) it employs simplified two-stage chemical reactions to calculate reaction heat and gas generation rates, reducing the number of fitting parameters without sacrificing accuracy; (2) it accounts for changes in the battery's void volume following the ejection of sparks from the cell.

For the lumped model, the energy conservation of the cell can be written by
(1)
C
p , cell
m cell
d
T cell
dt
=
Q ̇
app
+
Q ̇
gen
−
Q ̇
diss
where C p,cell, m cell, and T cell are the specific heat, mass, and temperature of the cell, respectively.

Q ̇
app
is applied heat power to the cell, and
Q ̇
gen
represents the heat generation rate of exothermic chemical reactions given by
(2)
Q ̇
gen
=
∑ i
Q ̇
i
where
Q ̇
i
(i = 1, 2) denotes the heat generation rate at each stage. In this work, the whole process of heat generation was divided into two stages. The heat generation in the first stage is dominated by solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) decomposition, the reaction of intercalated lithium with electrolyte at the anode. The heat generation in the second stage is mainly attributed to electrolyte decomposition, the cathode material decomposition, the reaction between anode and cathode, the reaction of released oxygen with electrolyte and binder and ohmic heat generated by large scale internal short circuit. The chemical reaction sequence has been reported recently using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) tests with individual and combined cell components [29–31]. In ARC testing, the cell is tested intact to study its thermal stability, while the cell components are used in DSC tests. The separator plays a key role in a cell, namely physically inhibiting electrical contact between the cathode and the anode. The DSC tests with cell components (cathode and anode materials) are performed without considering the influence of the insulating barrier of the separator. In addition, the electrolyte will evaporate and be released after the venting disk is open, that is, the electrolyte reacted with active materials is much less than given by the original value. These two factors warrant further study but is beyond the scope of this work. In this work, we used ARC tests to extract the reaction kinetic parameters. In this approach, the chemical reaction sequence cannot be clearly described as these chemical reactions are overlapped.

It is known that the heat generation at the first stage is small compared to the second stage. The latter is slow but accelerates rapidly in the final stage. The heat generation rate can be written as [32].
(3)
Q ̇
i
=
m cell
H i
d
x i
dt
(4)
d
x i
dt
=
1 −
x i
n i
A i
exp
−
E i
R
T cell
where H
i is the reaction heat per unit mass, x
i is the fractional degree of conversion with an initial value of 0.01, n
i is the reaction order, A
i the pre-exponential factor for the reaction, E
i the activation energy for the reaction, and R the universal gas constant. This simplified chemical reaction model can match well with the surface temperature of ARC tests and has been validated in previous work [32].

Q ̇
diss
is the heat dissipation rate calculated by
(5)
Q ̇
diss
=
A cell
h cell
T cell
−
T amb
+ εσ
T cell 4
−
T amb 4
where h cell is the equivalent heat transfer coefficient, A cell is the surface area of the cell, T amb is the ambient temperature, ε is the surface emissivity, and σ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

During the whole thermal runaway process, the mass conservation is applicable to all the components before and after venting. The mass conservation of the cell can be given as
(6)

m
solid , vent
dt
where m gas,vent, and m solid,vent are mass of the gas mixture, and solid ejecta ejected from the cell after venting. The first term on the left side is the rate of change of the mass of the cell. The first term on the right side is the discharge rate of the gas mixture, and the second term on the right is the eruption rate of solids ejected. It was observed from the experiments that the solid ejecta was only ejected after sparks and before jet flame. In the simulation, this term was activated when the cell surface temperature exceeded 470 K, at which point sparks were observed to eject from the cell. The last term on the right side can be calculated as follows:
(7)

The internal pressure of the cell includes two parts, one is from electrolyte vapour and another part is from the gas mixture generated by chemical reaction during the thermal runaway process. Therefore, the internal pressure of the cell, P cell, can be written as [13,14].
(13)
P cell
=
P vap
+
P gas
where P vap denotes the partial pressure contributed by the electrolyte vapour, and P gas is the partial pressure contributed by other gas mixtures. Assume that the equation of the state of all gases inside the cell follows the ideal gas law. Therefore, P vap and P gas can be written as
(14)
P vap

The rate of change of the mass of electrolyte vapour, dm vap/dt, can be calculated by Eq. (9) and the rate of change of the mass of gas mixture, dm gas/dt, can be calculated by Eq. (11), respectively. And the rate of change of the cell temperature, dT cell/dt, can be calculated by Eq. (1). The void volume, V void, will increase with solid components ejected from the cell, which has been verified by the postmortem analysis such as X-ray computed tomography (CT) scan [4]. However, the exact relationship between the void volume and the mass of ejected solid mass is still unknown. Since the chemical reaction is closely related to the internal pressure, the model assumes that the relation of void volume and mass of ejected solid components is followed by
(19)
d
V void
dt
=
m
out , solid
ρ solid
d
x m
dt
where ρ solid is the density of ejected solid components.

As the pressure builds up inside the cell, the venting disc will open when the internal pressure exceeds the threshold of pressure of the vent disc (P valve). The gases are released from the opening of the vent disc. Before the venting disc opens, the electrolyte and its vapour are in a state of vapour-liquid equilibrium (VLE). The saturation pressure of the electrolyte vapour, P sat, is equal to the partial pressure contributed by the electrolyte vapour, that is, P sat = P vap, and the relationship between saturation pressure and temperature can be described by Antoine's equation given as [13]:
(20)
P sat
=
10
a −
b
T cell
+ c
where a, b, and c are constants. After the venting disc opens, the Eq. (20) is no longer applicable. The state of gas flow via the opening can be considered as an isentropic process and it can be determined according to the critical pressure ratio (CPR), given as [13]:
(21)
CPR =
P amb
P cell
=
2
k + 1
k
k − 1
where P amb is the absolute ambient pressure and κ is the heat capacity ratio of the gas mixture.

The gas flow is subcritical if P
vent /P cell > CPR, and otherwise, it is a critical flow. The rate of change of mass flow can be calculated by

0.82

And u vent, P vent, and T vent are the speed, pressure, temperature at the interface of the vent disc, respectively, and define as [13].

Note that Eqs. (24)–(26) also applies before the vent disc opens, that is, the pressure and temperature at the interface of the vent disc are equal to the temperature and pressure of the cell before the vent disc opens. Eqs. (22) and (24)–(27) are derived based on principles of the energy balance for an ideal gas through a nozzle under the assumption of one-dimensional isentropic flow [33].

Before the vent disc is open, the mass of electrolyte vapour can be calculated using Eq. (15), therefore, the initial mass of electrolyte vapour, m vap,0, is
(28)
m
vap , 0
=
M vap
P
vap , 0
V void
R
T
cell , 0
where P vap,0 is the initial pressure of the electrolyte vapour, calculated by the Eq. (20), and T
cell,0 is the initial temperature, measured by a thermocouple on the cell surface. The initial mass of gas mixture is calculated as
(29)
m
gas , 0
=
M gas
P
cell , 0
−
P
vap , 0
V void
R
T
cell , 0
where P cell,0 is the initial pressure of the gas mixture, measured by the pressure sensor.

The current model has been implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics 6.0®. The implicit backward differentiation formula (BDF) solver with five orders of accuracy, maximum fixed time step and the fully coupled approach are used in all simulations. The relative tolerance of 1.0e−9 is used in all simulations to keep the accuracy of the model.

Three tests were conducted with an ARC. The first cell was not instrumented with a sensor, while the second and the third cells were equipped with a pressure sensor and a temperature sensor, respectively. The test results are listed in Table 3 and the internal temperature increases to as high as 1008.9 °C. The mass loss ratio of all tests is over 50 %, that is, more than half of cell components are ejected during testing. The maximum surface temperature of Test 2 is lower than the others, possibly because the thermocouple became loose during the experimental programme. The pressure results in Test 2 are not shown here because the pressure sensor was not operational under the high temperatures of the ARC test and failed to record reasonable pressure measurements during the test.

Fig. 4 shows the surface temperature and temperature rise rate of the cells. The self-heating temperatures were found to be 101.8 °C, 106.6 °C, and 111.6 °C with a detected temperature increase rate of 0.023 °C/min, 0.024 °C/min, and 0.025 °C/min for Tests 1–3, respectively. The cell will be at the point of self-heating once the ARC detects that the temperature rise rate exceeds the given sensitivity of the temperature rise rate detection limit (0.02 °C/min). The temperature drop of about 5 °C following soft venting in Test 1 is observed, while the temperature drops in Tests 2 and 3 are not obvious within the data collected. This can be seen from locally enlarged figures in Fig. 4a, b, and c.

Fig. 5 shows the comparison between surface temperature, internal temperature and their temperature rise rate in Test 3. As shown in Fig. 5a, the internal temperature and the surface temperature overlap before thermal runaway. The surface temperature is higher than the internal temperature at the initial heating stage due to the application of external heating. The internal temperature is slightly higher than or equal to the surface temperature since the cell starts to self-heat. The difference between the internal temperature and surface temperature increases gradually when the surface temperature is over 147 °C, and reaches the maximum during thermal runaway shown in Fig. 5b. The linear interpolation was used to calculate the temperature difference due to mismatched sampling rates between ARC and the data logger for recording the internal pressure. Note that the sampling rate is 10 Hz for the data logger. As shown in Fig. 5c, the most of temperature rise rate of the internal temperature fluctuates between −0.33 °C/s and 0.33 °C/s before the cell goes into the self-heating stage because the slight temperature variations can lead to significant fluctuations in the rate of temperature rise due to relatively high sampling rate (10 Hz). For example, if the difference between two sampling points is 0.01 °C, the rate of temperature rise will be 0.1 °C/s. The fluctuation in the rate of temperature rise persists until the cell goes into thermal runaway. As shown in Fig. 5d, there are two gaps marked by an orange rectangle for the rate of internal temperature change, which means that the fast drop in the internal temperature leads to a decrease in the rate of surface temperature change. For example, the internal temperature drops to 300.02 °C from 340.65 °C in 0.4 s and then increases to 441.54 °C in 0.1 s, and after about 9 s, the rate of temperature rise on the cell surface drops to 2.97 °C/s from 19.13 °C/s.

Three external heating tests were conducted to investigate the internal pressure evolution of the cells under test. As shown in Fig. 6 , the internal pressure increases gradually at the initial stage and begins to accelerate when internal gas generation increases until reaching the pressure threshold of the vent disc. After this, the internal pressure drops rapidly to ambient pressure due to the breaking of the vent disc. At the same time, the surface temperature drops due to the evaporation of electrolytes and the release of electrolyte vapour. The internal pressure increases rapidly when the cell goes into thermal runaway. The summary of peak pressures is listed in Table 4 . The average of the first peak pressure is 20.43 bar, and the second one is 18.27 bar, respectively. This means that the pressure threshold of the vent disc is about 20.43 bar, which is very close to the test result of about 2.0 MPa for 18,650-format cells reported in Ref. [34]. Note that the second pressure only lasts <0.5 s from the beginning of the pressure increase to the peak value, and then the pressure drops to below ambient pressure. The recorded pressures less than ambient pressure are not adopted because the pressure sensor is not calibrated below ambient pressure. The possible reason is that the temperature of hot gases filled in the pressure tube has exceeded the operating temperature of the pressure sensor and the pressure sensor is not working normally under these high temperatures. As shown in Fig. 6d-f, the cell voltage drops to close to 2 V from 4.18 V before the venting disk is open, and then the cell voltage abruptly drops to about 0.5 V when the venting disk is open. Following this, the cell voltage recovers somewhat until thermal runaway happens and finally drops to 0 V. The drop in cell voltage may be attributed to both the increase in the internal resistance at elevated temperatures [35] and the internal short circuit caused by shrinkage and even melting down of the separator under such high temperatures. The large internal resistance and the small internal short-circuit current can lead to a large voltage drop and contribute a little Joule heat due to the small current. The cell voltage recovers temporarily during the test possibly because the internal short-circuit path disappears temporarily.

Fig. 7 shows the characteristics of gases, sparks, and flame during each thermal runaway event. It is a snapshot from the video recording of Test 5 as an example. The full video can be seen in Appendix A (Supplementary material). There are no visible changes before the opening of the vent disc. A loud sound caused by the sudden release of high-pressure gas can be heard when the internal pressure exceeds the pressure threshold of the vent disc. After venting, white smoke is released from the vent holes for a few seconds, followed by the ejection of sparks and fire from the vent holes and the bottom vent, as the cell contains two pressure relief vents. A jet flame can be observed transforming into a low-momentum flame, indicating a gradual reduction in internal pressure during the process. Then, the fire gradually extinguished, and the cell cooled to ambient temperature.

Fig. 7 shows the characteristics of gases, sparks, and flame during each thermal runaway event. It is a snapshot from the video recording of Test 5 as an example. The full video is available in Supplementary Material. There are no visible changes before the opening of the vent disc. A loud sound caused by the sudden release of high-pressure gas can be heard when the internal pressure exceeds the pressure threshold of the vent disc. After venting, white smoke is released from the vent holes for a few seconds, followed by the ejection of sparks and fire from the vent holes and the bottom vent, as the cell contains two pressure relief vents. A jet flame can be observed transforming into a low-momentum flame, indicating a gradual reduction in internal pressure during the process. Then, the fire gradually extinguished, and the cell cooled to ambient temperature.

Test 6 is an example to validate the model. The cell side surface is assumed to insulate heat to the environment due to the insulating wrapping material. Fig. 8a shows a comparison of surface temperature and internal pressure between the test and the model. The model can match the test results well. The simulation results show that the time points of the first and second peak pressures are 2401.38 s and 2466.10 s, respectively, with an absolute relative error of less than 0.5% compared to the experimental results. The first and second peak pressures are 20.43 bar and 30.79 bar, respectively, with an absolute relative error of less than 3% in relation to the experimental results. The model parameters are listed in Table 5 .

To match the curve of temperature and internal pressure, the gas generation at stages I and II and the coefficient of heat power applied to the cell surface are fitted. Kinetic parameters of chemical reactions, see Table 6 , were extracted based on the ARC Test 1. The detailed method to extract these parameters can be found in our previous work [32].

Fig. 9 illustrates the battery morphologies following thermal runaway. In these tests, no sidewall ruptures were observed as excess internal pressure was released through the bottom vent and vent holes. The edges of the top cover (positive terminal) exhibit irregularities, with portions melted under high temperatures. As described in Section 2.1, the two disconnected semicircular scorings at the cell base represent structurally weaker areas, leading to ruptures at these points. The cell bottom shows notable bulging, and the enlarged vent opening facilitates pressure release. As shown in Fig. 9a, Test 4 shows more severe thermal runaway behaviour than other tests, reflected in a higher mass loss ratio and the extensive melted edges around the opening. The mass loss in Test 4 is higher than in Tests 5 and 6 likely due to a larger vent area. However, due to the limited number of experiments, a definitive correlation between vent area and mass loss ratio remains inconclusive. Table 7 shows the measured vent areas after thermal runaway. The difference in vent holes' area is not significant in each test. The bottom vent is open when the internal pressure is greater than its threshold. As presented in Fig. 7, the cell components will eject from the bottom vent once it is open, which is later than the opening of the vent disc.

As can be seen in Table 7, the area of the bottom vent is 1.3 to 3.5 times larger than vent holes. The average total vent area is 40.0 mm2. To investigate the influence of the vent area on internal pressure, three vent areas were used based on the averaged value of measurements. The first peak pressure is not influenced by the venting areas because the venting disk is closed before reaching the first peak pressure. As shown in Fig. 8b, the second peak pressure increases as the total vent area decreases because the gas flow rate increases with the increasing vent area. The smaller the vent area, the more gas will accumulate inside the cell and the higher the second peak pressure. The internal peak pressure drops to 28.2 bar from 31.8 bar when the total vent area increases to 60 mm2 from 20 mm2. In real tests, the internal pressure is affected by many factors, which will generally always jointly affect the internal pressure. This phenomenon is further discussed in Section 4.5.

Fig. 8c shows the effect of gas generation at stage II on the internal pressure. The internal peak pressure increases as the gas generation increases during thermal runaway. The venting disc will open earlier if the gas generation increases. For example, when the amount of gas generation in stage II increases to 21 mmol from 10.5 mmol, the second peak pressure increases to 6.3 bar from 3.1 bar and the time of vent disc opens approximately 15.9 s earlier. According to tests conducted by Lao et al. [3], the tensile strength of battery casing decreases significantly with increasing temperature, recorded at 101.30 MPa at 600 °C and 28.91 at 800 °C. If the vent area is not enough to release the internal high pressure, the cell will be at a high risk of sidewall rupture. Based on the recent experiments by Mao et al. [36] on gas generation and battery temperature rise rate, it is reasonable for the assumption that the rate of gas generation is proportional to the rate of heat generation in some models [14]. However, the gas production at each stage needs to be further investigated because the in-situ gas components and gas production during the thermal runaway test are still not well defined in the literature.

The internal pressure is influenced by many factors such as vent area, gas generation rate, void volume, and heating rate. The increase in gas generation and the decrease in vent area and void volume would theoretically increase the internal pressure. However, the process of thermal runaway is very complicated. The three phases including the gas phase, liquid phase, and solid phase exist at the stage from sparks to jet flame ignition. The cell components are ejected from the opening of the cell under high internal pressure. A large void will be generated at the same time. Coupled with the combustion of cell components and the movement of cell components towards the outside induced by internal pressure, the internal structure of the cell will change. Therefore, the internal pressure, especially at the stage of thermal runaway, changes rapidly due to the sudden change in the vent area and change of internal structure. It is difficult to get repeatable results for the second peak pressure even using the same heating conditions.

Venting holes are critical for relieving internal pressure, with the venting valve activating once internal pressure exceeds its threshold. Fig. 10 shows the impact of valve pressure on internal cell pressure. Fig. 10b is an enlarged view of Fig. 10a for the time interval 2200 – 2600 s. The effect on the second peak pressure is negligible as the valve pressure primarily influences the opening timing of the safety valve. Specifically, venting occurs earlier than 12.7 s when valve pressure decreases to 1.9 MPa from 2.043 MPa, and later than 4.3 s when valve pressure increases to 2.1 MPa. The bottom vent further assists in releasing excess internal pressure. Cell components and internal pressure release both occur from the bottom vent and the vent holes on the top cover. More cell components will be ejected from both vents than through only the vent holes because the total area of the vent holes is smaller than that of the bottom vent. Additionally, the vent holes have been observed to be occasionally blocked by cell components, further limiting ejection through them. The use of both vents will reduce the risk of sidewall rupture when a large pressure is generated inside the cell and decrease the heat transfer directly from flame radiation to the adjacent cells.

The internal pressure is also influenced by the cell's shape, active materials, and state of charge (SoC). For instance, pouch cells and prismatic cells may demonstrate different internal pressure evolution behaviours compared to cylindrical cells due to varying internal space volume. Cells with a low SoC and lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells may exhibit milder internal pressure characteristics. Furthermore, electrolyte evaporation plays a significant role in pressure buildup and requires further characterisation. By collecting electrolyte vapour and quantifying the evaporation rate during thermal runaway, an electrolyte evaporation model could be incorporated to enhance the accuracy of the model.

The evolution of the internal pressure of 21,700-format lithium-ion cells under thermal abuse conditions was studied by both experimental and numerical modelling. The experimental results on three external heating tests demonstrate that the cell's internal pressure after soft venting is significantly influenced by multiple factors such as the vent area. A lumped model was developed and validated based on testing parameters, with the strength of considering both two-stage chemical reactions and internal void change of the cell during thermal runaway. Two key influencing factors, the vent area and gas generation, were investigated with the model, showing that both increasing the vent area and decreasing gas generation can reduce internal pressure. This research confirmed that the bottom vent can be considered a safety strategy to release internal pressure as well as reduce the risk of sidewall rupture. The developed model can be extended to study the evolution of internal pressure in 4680-format or large-format prismatic lithium-ion cells and battery fire modelling, along with combining the internal pressure measurements and in-situ gas analysis.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary Video 1
Full footage of Test 5.
Supplementary Video 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2025.116066.

Haodong Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Begum Gulsoy: Investigation, Data curation. Anup Barai: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Puritut Nakhanivej: Investigation, Data curation. Melanie J. Loveridge: Writing – review & editing. James Marco: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
